text.title: Fork vs Fix — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules governing axis scoring, action selection, and the discipline of forking.

rule.statement: Decision MUST score 5 axes (0-3 each, 15 total): URGENCY (3=hours, 0=weeks-OK), BLAST (3=CI/build broken, 0=one route), PATCH-SIZE (3=>100 LOC, 0=<10 LOC), UPSTREAM-RESPONSIVENESS (3=dead repo, 0=PR-merged-in-days), STRATEGIC-DEPENDENCY (3=core forever, 0=we're replacing it next quarter).

rationale.source: NERO n8n fork case study + Tom Critchlow forking essays

rule.rationale: Single-axis decisions ("just fork it") create maintenance debt; the 5-axis score forces explicit trade-off across speed, scope, effort, and strategic horizon.

rule.statement: Action selection from total score: 0-3 → WORKAROUND (config / wrap); 4-6 → MONKEY-PATCH (patch-package, contained); 7-9 → UPSTREAM-PR (with workaround until merged); 10-12 → FORK-PIN (private fork until upstream catches up); 13-15 → REPLACE (swap for alternative library).

rationale.source: OSS contribution analytics 2020-2024 + agency observed patterns

rule.rationale: Score bands map to the empirical pain/benefit of each path. Below 7 forking is over-investment; above 12 the library is so broken it's safer to leave.

rule.statement: STRATEGIC-DEPENDENCY score == 3 forces action ≥ UPSTREAM-PR (never WORKAROUND or MONKEY-PATCH alone). If the library is core forever, we must invest in shaping its trajectory, not patch around it.

rationale.source: n8n fork example (NERO) + Patrick McKenzie kalzumeus

rule.rationale: If a library is truly forever-core (Rails, Postgres, n8n for an automation product), patches accumulate to unmaintainable scar tissue. Upstream PR is the only durable strategy.

rule.statement: FORK-PIN action MUST emit a playbook with: (a) fork repo URL, (b) branch naming `vX-faion-fork`, (c) Renovate config to track upstream changes, (d) re-rebase cadence (≥ monthly), (e) sunset criterion ("when upstream merges X" OR "by date Y"). Forks without sunset criteria become orphan codebases.

rationale.source: GitHub fork-graveyard analytics + observed agency anti-pattern

rule.rationale: Forks created in heat-of-incident mode and never re-rebased become unmaintainable within 12-18 months. Sunset criteria force the team to either upstream the patch or commit to long-term maintenance budget.

rule.statement: MONKEY-PATCH is FORBIDDEN for: security-critical libraries (auth, crypto, validation), core framework runtimes (React, Next.js core), and any library shipping its own update mechanism. For these, only WORKAROUND or UPSTREAM-PR or FORK-PIN.

rationale.source: OWASP supply-chain advisories / Heroku monkey-patch incident reports

rule.rationale: Monkey-patches on security libraries silently revert on dep upgrade and create undocumented attack surface. Framework runtime patches break on minor bumps. Ban-list prevents catastrophic categories.

example.title: NERO n8n fork case (real-world)
example.code:
Library: n8n (workflow automation engine) Bug: Custom node loader does not support our Python-via-child_process pattern; we need to fork to ship the integration.
Axis scoring: URGENCY = 2 (days, not hours) BLAST = 3 (blocks entire product line) PATCH-SIZE = 2 (~40 LOC across 3 files) UPSTREAM-RESPONSIVENESS = 2 (median merge: 3 weeks; mixed) STRATEGIC-DEPENDENCY = 3 (n8n is core forever) Total = 12
Action: FORK-PIN (band 10-12) + Upstream PR opened in parallel (per r3 strategic override) + Branch: v1.x-faion-fork + Renovate tracks upstream weekly + Sunset: when our PR merges OR by 2027-Q1
example.note: r3 forces parallel upstream PR despite FORK-PIN action. Hybrid path.

example.title: Monkey-patch on auth library (r5 violation)
example.code:
Library: next-auth Bug: Token expiry off by 30s; quick fix in node_modules/...
Engineer's plan: patch-package next-auth/server/jwt.js, ship.
r5 BAN: next-auth is auth-critical → MONKEY-PATCH forbidden. Re-route to: WORKAROUND (configure 30s tolerance via valid config) OR UPSTREAM-PR (with bridging workaround)
example.note: Even when monkey-patch is fastest, security boundary forces the slower path.
